text: The surgeon begins the procedure by placing ports for laparoscopic access, marking the initial step in the prostatectomy.
The surgical team adjusts the patient to a Trendelenburg position, tilting the operating table to lower the head and elevate the feet, facilitating better access to the pelvic region.
The surgeon performs a mini laparotomy above the umbilicus, creating a small incision in the abdominal wall.
The surgeon approaches the endopelvic fascia, identifying small vessels in the area. The same procedure is performed on the opposite side.
The surgeon requests an increase in the power of the bipolar electrocautery device while attempting to coagulate a large vein visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to clean the surface of the prostate by removing surrounding fat tissue. The bladder is checked for emptiness, with a request to empty it if necessary.

The surgeon utilizes the fourth arm of the robotic system to assist in manipulating the endopelvic fascia. The optic lens is cleaned to improve visibility, and the bladder is emptied to facilitate the procedure.
The surgeon begins the prostatectomy with the bladder emptied. The bladder neck is identified, and the overlapping bladder and prostate are visible. Fat tissue is removed from the surgical field.

The surgeon uses an instrument to palpate and differentiate between the mobile bladder neck and the solid prostate, demonstrating a technique to identify the anatomical boundary between these structures.
The surgeon uses a ProGrasp instrument to apply traction, revealing the bladder neck. Longitudinal muscle fibers are visible running into the prostatic urethra as the tissue is manipulated.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection around the urethra, extending the dissection laterally while maintaining a safe distance from the nearby nerves.
The surgeon is working laterally, preserving the bladder neck during the prostatectomy. The surgical field shows the bladder neck clearly surrounded by dissected tissue.
The surgeon opens the bladder neck, making a precise incision while the catheter is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates a conflict of instruments while identifying the correct surgical plane, avoiding entry into the adenomectomy plane. The precise dissection continues at the appropriate level.
The surgeon locates the tips of the seminal vesicles, noting their elongated appearance.

The surgeon performs a wide excision using electrocautery, with visible cauterization of tissue. The procedure involves clipping the vas deferens, and the surgical field includes small vessels requiring a clip. The lens is cleaned to maintain clear visibility of the surgical area.

The surgeon applies small metal clips to secure small vessels, using 5mm clips for effective hemostasis. The tips of the seminal vesicles are visible, with some groups opting to leave them inside, which is considered oncologically safe.
The surgeon places a metal clip on the seminal structure located on the left side, securing it during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon moves to the opposite side and identifies the Danube's Fossa, applying downward pressure to manipulate the tissue.
The surgeon identifies two large arteries supplying the seminal vesicles, highlighting their prominence in the surgical field.
The surgeon applies a metal clip to a vessel, securing it in place.

The surgeon gains access to the Denonvilliers' fascia, identifying the intrafascial plane and performing a wide excision on the right side. The incision is made directly on the capsule, revealing the Denonvilliers' fascia still attached to the pre-rectal fatty tissue. The surgeon proceeds with a lateral cut.
The surgeon opens the Denonvilliers' fascia on one side, carefully navigating medially to the pulvostatic ligament to preserve it. During this process, a vein is inadvertently opened.

The surgeon discusses the use of a clip to control bleeding, instructing the assistant to apply a metal clip temporarily, which will be removed during the apical dissection. The surgeon emphasizes the importance of not leaving the clip in place to prevent migration into the anastomosis.
The surgeon requests a 4-0 Vicryl suture to address a large opening in a vein, visible in the surgical field. The focus is on nerve sparing during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a needle holder to place a stitch at the apex, effectively stopping the bleeding from the opened vein.
The surgeon uses scissors to address a small area of oozing, attempting to control the bleeding by applying another stitch.

The surgeon addresses a bleeding site, applying a hemostatic technique to stop the bleeding. Once the bleeding is controlled, the surgeon proceeds to cut and remove the tissue, allowing the procedure to continue.

The surgeon performs a lateral opening, carefully manipulating the novus fascia by pushing it down while staying close to the capsule. On one side, interfacial nerve sparing is performed, while a wider excision is executed on the other side.

The surgeon adjusts the view using a third-degree angle, cleans the lens, and repositions the bladder downward to improve access to the area behind it. The procedure begins with a monolateral nerve-sparing radical prostatectomy, starting from the side where nerve sparing is not required, using a hemolog clip.
The surgeon uses a hemolog clip to secure tissue, creating more space for a clear view of the posterior side. The optic is adjusted to enhance visualization.

The surgeon adjusts the optic to improve the view of the prostate apex, revealing a clearer dissection plane. The surgical field is closely examined, highlighting the anatomical plane and surrounding structures.

The surgeon is performing intrafacial dissection, focusing on nerve preservation on the right side. The orientation of the prostate capsule is clearly visible, allowing for precise placement of a clip.

The surgeon creates space near the apex of the prostate and places a clip. As they approach the apex, clips are no longer needed, and the structures are peeled off. The interfacial nerve-sparing technique is used, where nerves are covered and not visible, revealing a thick structure.
The surgeon identifies a small vessel and applies a clip to secure it, ensuring the surrounding nerves remain intact. The nerves are visible both dorsolaterally and laterally in the surgical field.
The surgeon adjusts the optic to achieve a clear view of the surgical field, turning it ventrally and laterally to facilitate the identification and creation of the dissection plane.

The surgeon identifies the ligament medially and notes a large vein similar to the opposite side. The tissue is completely peeled off, and the surgeon prepares to stop minor venous bleeding with a stitch. The request is made to increase gas pressure temporarily to manage the situation.

The surgeon ensures a clear view by controlling bleeding during the dissection. The neurovascular bundle and nerves are visible, covered by a thin layer of periprostatic fascia, which is no longer on the surface of the prostate.

The surgeon demonstrates a planar dissection of the anatomical structures, revealing veins under the fascia along with nerves and an artery. The surgical field is clear and well-controlled, showcasing successful dissection before proceeding to the opposite side.
The surgeon continues working within the endoperic fissure, using scissors to cut tissue and applying a clip to secure a small vessel.

The surgeon adjusts the optic to enhance visibility, revealing the prostate and the pedicle. Clips are applied to manage adhesions, but the tissue is too adhesive, prompting a pause to improve visibility.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to cut through tissue, revealing a clear interfacial plane. Clips are applied to secure structures, and the surgeon attempts to move more laterally on the right side due to the presence of a Gleason dominant fork. Emolox clips are used frequently, potentially offering less trauma compared to bipolar coagulation.

The surgeon uses Hem-o-lok clips to preserve nerves during the prostatectomy, avoiding the use of electrocautery to minimize trauma. The nerves are visible and partly preserved, demonstrating careful handling to prevent damage.

The surgeon requests cleaning of the lens to improve visibility. The prostate is nearly completely mobilized, revealing a bulky structure with some small nerves remaining. The optic is turned to fully expose the apex of the prostate.
The surgeon approaches the apex of the prostate, reaching the urethra, with the area now completely free of surrounding tissue.

The surgeon adjusts the camera to a thirty-degree downward angle to perform the apical dissection of the prostate. There is minor bleeding observed, and the surgeon follows the prostate's anatomy, attempting to mobilize the lateral tissues as much as possible.
The surgeon identifies a superficial vein and requests cleaning of the optic lens. A preference for stitching is expressed, and the pressure is increased to eighteen for a moment.
The surgeon requests a needle holder and uses suction to clear the area. They proceed to stitch under the ligaments on one side, preparing to perform the same action on the opposite side.
The surgeon stitches the wings, ensuring that the ligaments remain superficial and are not included in the stitch.

The surgeon uses scissors to cut tissue at the apex of the prostate, followed by suction to clear the surgical field. The apex is carefully examined to distinguish between the urethra and prostate, especially challenging in small prostates.

The surgeon uses suction to clear the surgical field, revealing the mobilized prostate. The apex is carefully developed step by step, avoiding excessive energy use to prevent damage to the sphincter fibers and associated nerves.

The surgeon prepares to suture the inner layer of the urethra to the bladder, performing an anastomosis. The visual shows the end of the prostate and the plane where the dissection is occurring, highlighting the importance of avoiding leaving parts of the prostate inside by cutting in the correct plane.

The surgeon performs the same procedure on the opposite side, applying suction while maintaining tension on the prostate to facilitate dissection. The prostate is held firmly to ensure proper tension during this step.

The surgeon identifies open veins near the anastomosis site, indicating that the previous stitch was not deep enough. A running suture will be applied to stop the bleeding after completing the cutting. The urethra will be reconstructed using a two-layer Rocco stitch, followed by a running anastomosis with a Frio strata fix.
The surgeon exposes open veins near the prostate apex, which are not ligated. The veins are visible, and careful handling is emphasized to avoid deep stitching that could damage the sphincter.

The surgeon identifies longitudinal muscle cell fibers similar to those at the bladder neck, aiming to dissect further to create a longer urethral stump. The catheter is visible, and large veins are observed, indicating that the cutting line is not horizontal.

The surgeon repositions the catheter and identifies the varomentanum deep inside the surgical field, noting the small size of the prostate. The varomentanum is visible and should be included in the specimen as the surgeon cuts distally to it.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the prostate, ensuring not to cut into the neurovascular bundle. The prostate is visible running along the surgical field, and the surgeon prepares to examine the specimen.
